content:
is it bad that I seriously, horrendously dislike the term "Dimensional Image"?
I really hope this was just picked due to MTL. I'd like to at least show the steps I take to try to understand Chinese words and see if I'm right or wrong in my approach to doing that... I'd like to try to glean a more smooth, if potentially less literal, term from the original 维度影像.

So my process is (ironically) to go to Google Translate and deconstruct the word. 维度影像 gives "Dimensional Imaging". cool. The next thing I do is go character by character and see what it gives.

- 维: "Dimension". Also gives a fleet of other words; maintain, thinking, preserve, safeguard, bind together, thought, hold together, tie up. What I get from this is that 维 roughly means "the *concept* of a space that holds things together".
- 度: "Degree"...? Also gives time, occasion, and "counter for occurrences". What I get from this is that it's here specifically to combine with another character, likely 维;
  - Together (维度): "Dimensions". Pretty sure this is what these two characters are here for. I understand this as roughly meaning "Space-time", used here specifically to call the idea that there could be *multiple distinct* space-times in our reality. Multiverse theory basically.
- 影: "Shadow". Also gives shade and "other side". What I get from this is that 影 probably doesn't literally mean *a shadow* like "the shadow cast by that tree", but more like "that tree's *shadow*" the way we use the term "dark side of the moon".
- 像: "Picture". Specifically, a representation, resemblance or lookalike. 
  - Together (影像): "Image", or "Portrait". I understand this to specifically mean *a depiction*; and not one that was created (like a painting), but one that was *captured* (like a photograph). 

So with this all put back together, what I understand this term 维度影像 to (probably) mean *IN CONTEXT* is this:
> A visual object, that accurately depicts not the "You" that exists, but the "You" that *could* exist in another reality; one that despite its impossible nature, undeniably *must* exist, as how could you capture such a picture with tools that can have no creative liberties? *The mere existence of this image demands reckoning with the fact that just as "you" are real to you and "they" are but a twisted reflection of your world, "they" are real to them, and "you" are but a twisted reflection of their world.*

I have not *read* "Pickman's Model" itself as I'm a brainrotted cultural swine with an allergy for primary sources, and I have not read any of the Morimens content surrounding the character of the same name, but from what I understand of Lovecraft's Pickman, I would not be surprised if Morimens' Pickman has something to do with these Relics.

That being said, am I off my rocker? Is the process I take to understand Chinese somehow procedurally incorrect? I am 100% open to having either of those be true, so let me know if that's the case. Bringing this full circle back to the top though, I have no idea what could possibly be a better term we could use for these, but I'm beyond confident "Dimensional Image" kinda sucks at conveying what it seems like it's supposed to. Thoughts?
that's
hmm
I admire the motivation here but that's not entirely quite how language works
trying to break down words into their component parts fails when words are just...words
there may be some ancient etymology which explains particular combinations of hanzi that combine to make specific words, but the level of dissection you're doing isn't really too valid
as a metaphor, it'd be like reading into the meaning of the english term "hotdog"
you can see it comprises of the word "hot", which must imply heat or spice, and the word "dog", which must imply something to do with dogs
therefore there must be an implication of, in some form, both spice and dog meat involved
(this is, obviously, not a valid conclusion)
rather than taking it character by character, 维度影像 is comprised of the two words 维度 and 影像, both of which are entire terms in their own
维度 is dimension, and yes it can mean multiverse and spacetime dimensions, but there's no explicit implication thereof
the term "extradimensional" in english can be used in a scientific context, but it can also be used in a fantastical context, a scifi context, a cosmic horror context, etc
影像 is image, but not necessarily a "captured depiction"
it could be any of image/portrait/reflection/hologram/shadow/illusion

different people will think of different things on reading it, same as how different people might get different mental images on hearing the term "dimensional image", but that's not to say there's an objective truthful portrayal communicable solely via the words themselves

it could be a literal image as in a photograph, it could be a portrait as in a painted media, it could be a projection in some hologram form, or it could be a shadow or vestige that's some echo of a dimensional mirror
basically, there's not really any conclusions you can make solely off the words used
we would need world setting info from the narrative designer to draw more concrete conclusions as to what exactly they are and what would be a more "correct" term to use
personally my own individual understanding has always been that they're some kind of dimensional projections but again, can't make any conclusions given the lack of detail provided
it's entirely possible they could be painted portraits of some sort or alternate universe doppelgängers that you're leeching power from

if I wanted to be fancy with it and had full creative liberty, I'd probably call it something like "Dimensional Vestige" but again, that's a big conclusion to draw off incomplete data and as it stands, the direct translation of dimensional image is probably the safest bet

> trying to break down words into their component parts fails when words are just...words
-# Except when words are portmanteaus 

> rather than taking it character by character, 维度影像 is comprised of the two words 维度 and 影像, both of which are entire terms in their own
Which is something I quickly realized, but only could have by breaking down the words further and discovering the point at which the meaning had dissolved.

> therefore there must be an implication of, in some form, both spice and dog meat involved 
I understand where you're coming from for sure, but what's glossed over in your argument that this is an invalid conclusion is the fact that "dog" here, from all we can glean from available written history, kinda *does* imply (cynically) that dog meat may have been involved in the creation of street sausages

ALL of which is completely irrelevant to the topic at hand, but thank you for helping me understand a bit better. I've said before and I'll say it again, my understanding of other languages basically stops at the adage "knows enough spanish to get him stabbed" so I'm doing my best lols
https://tenor.com/view/screaming-cat-browthy-gif-27553989

> I understand where you're coming from for sure, but what's glossed over in your argument that this is an invalid conclusion is the fact that "dog" here, from all we can glean from available written history, kinda *does* imply (cynically) that dog meat may have been involved in the creation of street sausages
yes, that's what I meant when I said that this might be relevant when it comes to etymology, because these words do have origins and *something* existed in the past to create these combinations which resulted in these words
but languages change over time and the etymological origin of a word from hundreds of years ago doesn't necessarily reflect how it's used nowadays

fair. I'm just aware that GTL knows about as much as to the meanings that could be hidden in these words as I do (that being, literally nothing), so I wanted to know if my making do with what I have available to me was sufficient.
ah, yes, indeed;
"维度" cannot be taken apart, like we kinda can't try to translate this character by character;
and about lore: there isn't really much lore haha... The combat designer needed a name, and the writers just suggested using the Awakener's profile image to save time and resources.
omg 🤣
imo the one issue with "Dimensional Image" is that it's a bit long, but overall it's functional so we're keeping it as is?
that's actually so cute in its own way

the *literally age-old* tale of fans reading more into a piece than the author. gotta love it <:PAIMON_DEAD:708170044848472105> 
My point stands, then, that "Dimensional Image" feels awkward and a bit of a mouthful in English, and I think it's reasonable that if we find a term that works better in context and in sentence structure, that it doesn't seem terribly important for it to be "Dimensional Image" *very specifically* the way that "Aliemus" MUST be "Aliemus" and nothing else?
like, i totally get why mercy may not like it; it's... pretty basic and straightforward as a translation; and as translators we may often want to polish phrases to make it look better;

This is actually a great example of a common principle we follow: the balance between polish and player clarity. While our instinct is to polish everything, sometimes changing established terminology, even if it's based on an old translation quirk or an internal typo, can cause more confusion than it's worth.

For instance, the community has been calling the item 'Dimensional Image' for a long time. Correcting the translation now would probably hurt readability more than it would help. 
The same goes for terms like 'Aliemus'. It was a typo from a narrative designer, and when the team first GPT'ed the whole game, they went with that. And when I joined everyone's been saying Aliemus since forever. So the typo's just stuck around because it's what players know! (also because it's not so obvious to the average person)
On the other hand it's great we're catching awkward phrases in like Stage Names because those are flavor texts

That's understandable. Thanks Ace. <:LUV:1003188300036702229> 
-# to be fair though, I'm not very active in the community so sample size is low but I've never heard anyone call them anything but "portrait"
> This is actually a great example of a common principle we follow: the balance between polish and player clarity. While our instinct is to polish everything, sometimes changing established terminology, even if it's based on an old translation quirk or an internal typo, can cause more confusion than it's worth.
"It's not common sense until you teach it", so thank you for teaching me.
